## **Part 1: GIT & GITHUB 101**

Git is your version control tool. GitHub is where you view and store your git projects on the web. 

### **\#1 Set Up**

**Step 1: Install Git**  
Check if Git is already installed by opening your terminal and typing git \--version. If you see a version number, you’re good.

If not:  
Go to git and select your operating system and follow instructions: [https://git-scm.com/install/](https://git-scm.com/install/)

**Step 2: Create a GitHub account**  
Sign up at github.com. Free accounts are fine for everything you need.

**Step 3: Install GitHub Desktop (recommended)**  
Download from desktop.github.com. GitHub Desktop gives you a visual interface for all your git operations. You’ll see your changes highlighted in green (additions) and red (deletions), and can commit and push with a click.

Sign into GitHub Desktop with your GitHub account and you’re connected.

### **\#2 Creating a repo & adding collaborators**

A repository ("repo") is your project’s home – it contains all your code, your history of changes, and your project management tools. Here’s how to set one up properly.

**Creating a new repository**  
On GitHub.com: click the “+” in the top right \>  New Repository. Or in GitHub Desktop: File \> New Repository.

**You’ll be asked for:**

* Repository name:  keep it short and descriptive (e.g. “my-app” or “acme-dashboard”)  
* Description:  one sentence about what the project is (optional but helpful)  
* Public vs Private: choose Private. More on this below.  
* Initialize with a README: if you check this box, it’ll create a starting file that describes your project.


**Always set your repo to Private**  
By default GitHub may suggest Public, which means anyone in the world can see your entire codebase. For a startup building a product or a vibe coder learning the rules of the game, you almost always want Private. If you ever had a repo public with API keys in it, make the repo private AND rotate/change those keys immediately.   
**Adding collaborators**  
Once your repo is created, you can give others access:

1. Go to your repo on GitHub.com  
2. Click Settings \> Collaborators (under “Access” in the sidebar)  
3. Click “Add people” and enter their GitHub username or email  
4. They’ll get an invite they need to accept

Collaborators get push access by default, meaning they can commit directly to the repo. If you want more control (e.g., requiring pull request reviews before merging to main), you can set up branch protection rules under Settings \> Branches.

### **\#3 Local versus Remote/Origin**

This is a core concept to understand. There are two copies of your project:

* **Local:**  the version on your computer, where you and Claude Code make edits. When you’re writing code in VS Code or testing on localhost, you’re working locally.  
* **Remote (aka “origin”):** the version on GitHub, stored in the cloud. This is the shared, backed-up copy that your team (and your deployment platform) can see.

Changes don’t automatically sync between the two. You have to explicitly push your local work up to the remote. A simple analogy: editing a local Word file vs. saving it to a shared SharePoint folder. Until you push, nobody else can see your changes and they’re not backed up.

**The basic flow**

1. Make changes locally (you \+ Claude Code)  
2. Test that everything works (run your app, check localhost)  
3. Commit (save a snapshot of your changes locally, with a message describing what changed that Claude Code can write for you)  
4. Push to origin (send those committed changes up to GitHub)

   *\> PRO TIP Commit often. Every time something works, commit it. This creates save points you can return to if a future change breaks things.*

### **\#4 Cloning: local copy of your GitHub repo**

Cloning means downloading a complete copy of a GitHub repository to your computer so you can work on it. This is how you start working on any project.

* In GitHub Desktop: File \> Clone Repository \> paste the repo URL or select from your list.   
* From the terminal: git clone https://github.com/your-username/your-repo.git

### **\#5 Forking: your own copy of someone else’s repo**

Forking means creating your own personal copy of someone else’s repository on GitHub. You’d do this if you want to experiment with an open-source project or start from a template. 

Click “Fork” on any GitHub repo page, then clone your fork to your machine.

### **\#6 Branches: work on features without breaking production**

Think of branches as parallel copies of your project. The main branch is your stable, production version – the code that’s live. When you want to build a new feature or make a big change, you create a new branch so you can experiment without risking the live version.

A simple branch approach (how we are using it): there is the ‘main’ branch, which is the stable, production branch. When you work on new features/changes, you create a ‘sub’ branch, and manually try your build, and ask Claude Code to run tests. You can also deploy that branch to Vercel (or wherever you’re deploying your app) to check deployment passes.

![][image1]

**The simple workflow (solo builder)**

1. You’re on the main branch.  
2. Ask Claude to create a new branch for your new feature  
3. Build and test on that new branch.  
4. Once the feature works, merge it back into main.

**Working with a team**

1. When you’re working with others/engineers, you don’t merge your branch directly to main.  
2. Instead, you open a Pull Request (more on that in the next section) so someone can review your code before it goes to production.  
   

**Merge conflicts**  
If multiple people/Claude Code sessions edit the same file on different branches, Git will flag a “merge conflict” when you try to combine them. Don’t panic\! Git shows you both versions and asks you to pick which one to keep (or combine them). Claude Code can help you resolve these.

So, you want to be mindful of isolating builds so you’re not changing the same files in multiple places or being prepared to deal with any conflicts.

### **\#7 Pull requests (PR): propose merging your branch to main** 

A Pull Request (PR) is how you propose merging your branch into main. It’s the central feature of team code review. When you open a PR, you’re saying: “Here are my changes –  please review them before they go to production.”

**The PR workflow**

1. Push your branch  
2. Go to your repo on GitHub: you’ll see a prompt to create a Pull Request  
3. Write a description of what changed and why  
4. An engineer/someone else on your team reviews the code, tests your build, and leaves feedback  
5. Once approved, you (or they) click “Merge” and your code goes to ‘main’

If you’re working solo, you can usually skip PRs and merge directly. 

### **\#8 .gitignore & protecting your secrets**

This is super important for safety\! Your project likely contains sensitive information such as API keys, database passwords, environment variables, that should never be pushed to GitHub. If your repo is public, anyone in the world can see those credentials and use them\! And even if it’s private, it’s bad practice.

**What is .gitignore?**

* A .gitignore file tells Git which files and folders to ignore – they won’t be tracked or pushed to GitHub. Every project should have one.  
* Example of what to include in .gitignore (Claude Code can do this for you)  
  * .env (this file is where your API keys, database URLs, and secrets live. What you’re doing by including this in gitignore is telling Git to ignore this .env file and its contents)  
  * .env.local, .env.production  
  * node\_modules/ (installed packages)  
  * .DS\_Store (Mac system files that clutter your repo)

  *\> PRO TIP Ask Claude Code to create a .gitignore for you when you start any project.*

### **\#9 Undoing mistakes**

Claude Code will sometimes break things. A feature will go sideways. You’ll want to go back to a state where everything worked.

**In Claude Code: Tell Claude to undo**  
Tell Claude Code to undo what it just did (the caveat: sometimes it can’t isolate changes well if you haven’t been committing your changes)

**In Claude Code: /rewind**  
Claude Code has a built-in “rewind” command that rolls your project back to an earlier state. Type /rewind and Claude will show you recent checkpoints to return to. This is the fastest way to undo a bad change.

**In GitHub Desktop: discard changes**  
If you haven’t committed yet and want to throw away your current changes, right-click the changed files in GitHub Desktop \> “Discard Changes.” This reverts them to the last commit.

\> PRO TIP  if something works, commit it immediately. The more frequently you commit, the more checkpoints you have to return to. Don’t go more than 30 minutes of working code without committing\!

### **\#10 Commit messages**

A commit message is a short description of what changed. When Claude Code commits for you, it writes the message automatically – and includes “co-authored-by” credit for both you and Claude.

If you’re committing manually in GitHub Desktop, write something descriptive:

* Good: “Add Stripe payment flow to checkout page”  
* Bad: “updated stuff”

Good commit messages save your future self. When something breaks and you’re scanning through history trying to figure out which change caused it, descriptive messages are super helpful\!

*If you commit locally in your GitHub Desktop app, you can write a manual message. You can also click on the icon in the bottom left and write with Copilot.*  
*![][image2]*

*You will see these commit messages in your Git repo (remote/origin) – see how the commit message says co-authored by me and Claude right under the Commit title.*   
***![][image3]***

### **\#11 Git Logs**

To see all changes made to your project, you have a few options:

1. **GitHub Desktop**: Click the “History” tab to see every commit with its diff.  
2. **GitHub.com**: Go to your repo \> click “Commits” to see the full history, including who made each change.  
3. **Terminal**: Run this command for a compact, visual history (see screenshot example):  
   1. git log \--graph \--oneline \--decorate \--all  
   2. It gives you line by line the changes made, with a description. There are other ways to get this info too depending on how you want to use it – you can ask Claude Code or Google Search. 

   **![][image4]**
